coredata.dc:title: Visual and ratiometric fluorescent determination of Al3+ by a red-emission carbon dot-quercetin system
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Baogang
Liu, Xing
Duan, Wenmeng
Dai, Shanshan
Lu, Hongsheng
coredata.dc:description: The red-emission carbon dots (RCDs) were facile synthesized by the solvothermal method to construct a visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe towards Al3+ in combined with quercetin (QCT). With the increasing concentration of Al3+ (c Al 3+), the red fluorescence (605 nm) of the RCDs–QCT system under 360 nm excitation was stable, and meanwhile a green fluorescence (506 nm) appeared and gradually enhanced because of the continuous formation of QCT–Al3+ complex. The red fluorescence served as the reference signal and the green as the reporter, and then a visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe towards Al3+ was established based on the variation of the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 506 and 605 nm (I 506/I 605) and the change of fluorescence color from red to yellow green. On account of the linear relationship between the I506/I605 value and c Al 3+, the detection range and limit of detection were determined to be 0–35 μM and 90 nM, respectively. The above Al3+ probe not only exhibited the high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interface ability, but also realized the quantitative determination of Al3+ in ultrapure and tap samples with satisfactory results, reflecting its great application potential for practical fluorescent determination of Al3+ in various aqueous environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Quercetin
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Al3+

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recovery tests in ultrapure and tap water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) high-resolution TEM images of RCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of RCD suspension (0.02 mg/mL) in ethanol. (b) FL spectra of RCD suspension (0.02 mg/mL) recorded under different excitation wavelengths. Inset of (b): photos of RCD suspension under the room light and UV light (365 nm).
Selectivity of RCD (0.02 mg/mL)-QCT (80 μM) system to various metal ions (50 μM).

(a) Fluorescence response of RCD (0.02 mg/mL)-QCT (80 μM) system upon addition of Al3+ (0–45 μM) under 360 nm excitation. Inset of (a): photos of RCD (0.02 mg/mL)-QCT (80 μM) systems with different c Al 3+ under 360 nm irradiation. (b) Plot of the I506/I605 value as a function of c Al 3+ (Three replicate measurements were conducted for each data point to gain the convincing mean values with error bar representing the standard deviation). Inset of (b): the fitted linear relationship between the I 506/I 605 value and c Al 3+.

(a) Fluorescence response of RCD (0.02 mg/mL)-QCT (80 μM) system (in tap water) upon addition of Al3+ (0–40 μM) under 360 nm excitation. (b) Plot of the I 506/I 605 value as a function of c Al 3+. Inset of (b): the fitted linear relationship between the I 506/I 605 value and c Al 3+.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of RCDs and their application in building a visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe of Al3+ in the presence of QCT.
Comparison of the performances of CD-based Al3+ fluorescent probes.
Recovery tests of spiked Al3+ in ultrapure and tap water samples by the probe based on the RCD-QCT system.

head.ce:title: Visual and ratiometric fluorescent determination of Al3+ by a red-emission carbon dot-quercetin system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Oil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Oil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bOil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Chengdu 610500, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The red-emission carbon dots (RCDs) were facile synthesized by the solvothermal method to construct a visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe towards Al3+ in combined with quercetin (QCT). With the increasing concentration of Al3+ (c Al 3+), the red fluorescence (605 nm) of the RCDs–QCT system under 360 nm excitation was stable, and meanwhile a green fluorescence (506 nm) appeared and gradually enhanced because of the continuous formation of QCT–Al3+ complex. The red fluorescence served as the reference signal and the green as the reporter, and then a visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe towards Al3+ was established based on the variation of the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 506 and 605 nm (I 506/I 605) and the change of fluorescence color from red to yellow green. On account of the linear relationship between the I506/I605 value and c Al 3+, the detection range and limit of detection were determined to be 0–35 μM and 90 nM, respectively. The above Al3+ probe not only exhibited the high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interface ability, but also realized the quantitative determination of Al3+ in ultrapure and tap samples with satisfactory results, reflecting its great application potential for practical fluorescent determination of Al3+ in various aqueous environments.

ce:section.ce:para: As the most abundant metallic element of earth crust, aluminum was widely used in our daily life and industrial fields. Al3+ is the most common existence form of aluminum element and excessive intake of Al3+ can harm the human health. Considering the possible relationship between Al3+ and Parkinson disease, Alzheimer's disease, dialysis encephalopathy and breast cancer [1–4], the World Health Organization allows that a maximum permissible level of Al3+ in drinking water is 7.41 μM [5]. Thus, it is of key importance to develop facile and accurate methods for quantitative determination of Al3+ in various aqueous environments.

Although a lot of methods such as the atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, high-performance liquid chromatography and electrochemical analysis for Al3+ determination were well built [6–9], the fluorescent probe has received considerable attention because of its high sensitivity, selectivity, low cost and real-time monitoring [10]. So far, most of fluorescent probes for Al3+ are based on organic molecules [11–14]. Several probes based on metal and semiconductor nanoparticles were also developed [15–17]. However, all of the above probes exhibited more or less disadvantages including the complicated synthetic routes, low photostability, high cost and toxicity, and poor water solubility. Hence, the recent carbon dot (CD)-based fluorescent probes for Al3+ are attracting more attention due to the tunable fluorescence property, high photostability, low cost, good biocompatibility and water solubility of CDs [18–29].

Compared with the single-signal probes [30–32], the ratiometric fluorescent probes, based on the detection of the ratio of fluorescence intensities under two well resolved wavelengths, exhibited the greatly improved detection accuracy and stability because their self-calibration function can effectively eliminate the influences derived from the fluctuation of excitation source, change of the local environment and variation of probe concentration [33–40]. Up to now, only three CD-based ratiometric fluorescent probes towards Al3+ have been established based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Zou et al. used blue-emission CD-quercetin (QCT) system to construct a Al3+ ratiometric fluorescent probe with the wide detection ranges (1–20 and 20–60 μM) and favorable limit of detection (LOD, 0.558 μM) [18]. However, Zn2+ results in interference for the Al3+ detection due to the formation of Zn2+–QCT complex. In addition, the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 429 and 481 nm (I429/I481) has been recorded despite the wavelength space between 429 and 481 nm was too narrow. Kim et al. built a Al3+ ratiometric fluorescent probe based on the rhodamine grafted CDs [19]. This probe can be used conveniently in the form of paper strip, whereas its LOD was too high (38 μM) and the Fe3+ exhibited the high interference. Kim et al. also used the poly(ethyleneglycol) to further modify the rhodamine grafted CDs [23]. Nevertheless, the Al3+ probe still suffered from some drawbacks such as the complicated synthesis procedures, very high LOD of 18 μM and weak anti-interference ability. Based on the dual-emission CDs, another Al3+ ratiometric fluorescent probe was established [20]. This probe had high selectivity to Al3+ over other metal ions, low LOD (1.2 μM) and relatively narrow wavelength space. According to the above description, it is easy to found that not only the quantity but also the performance of CD-based ratiometric fluorescent probes for Al3+ still urgently needs to be improved.

The above problems motivated us to do more promoting work on CD-based ratiometric fluorescent probes towards Al3+. On one hand, we hope to simplify the construction of probe, that is, to find a CDs with no need for further modification. On the other hand, we hope that the probe is not only based on the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer and possesses enhanced performance. As shown in Scheme 1, the red-emission CDs (RCDs) were synthesized by solvothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine in methylbenzene. The QCT, well known as one of common flavonols in nature with attractive anticancer, antiviral, antimutagenic and lipid peroxidation functions [41–44], was combined with RCDs to construct a visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe for Al3+. Upon 360 nm irradiation, the RCD–QCT system emitted red fluorescence at 605 nm. When the Al3+ was introduced to the above system, the red fluorescence was stable, whereas a bright green fluorescence at 506 nm appeared because of the formed Al3+–QCT complex. With the increasing concentration of Al3+ (c Al 3+), the I506/I605 value gradually increased, accompanied by the change of fluorescence color from red to yellow green. Then the visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe for Al3+ was established, which not only exhibited the high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability, but also realized the quantitative determination of Al3+ in ultrapure and tap water samples with satisfactory results

The QCT and p-phenylenediamine were purchased from the Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co, Ltd. (China). The analytical reagents including AlCl3, ZnCl2, NaCl, CrCl2, CoCl2, KCl, MnCl2, CuCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, H2HgN2O7, AgNO3, BaCl2, CaCl2, FeCl3, methylbenzene and ethanol were purchased from the Chengdu Kelong Chemical Regent Co, Ltd. (China). All of the above chemicals were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experimental process.

The RCDs were synthesized according to the reported method with a few modifications [45]. Typically, 0.1 g of p-phenylenediamine was added into 10 mL of toluene under stirring to form a transparent solution. The solution was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 185 ℃ for 5 h in an oven. After cooling down to the room temperature, the dark red supernatant was removed and the remaining solid was dissolved into 20 mL of ethanol. The obtained suspension was dialyzed by a dialysis bag (1000 Da) against frequently changed hot water (50 ℃) for 3 days. The dialyzate was filtrated by a filter membrane with pore diameter of 100 nm. Finally, the solvent of filtrate was eliminated by rotary evaporation and the RCDs were obtained as the brown powder.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a FEI Tecnai G20 system operating at 200 kV. The specimen was prepared by drop-casting the RCDs suspension (0.02 mg/mL) in ethanol onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on an X-ray diffractometer (X Pert PRO MPD) with a wavelength (λ) of 0.15418 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was gained on a FTIR spectrophotometer (WQF-520) by the KBr pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a mono-chromatized Al Kα X-ray source (1486.71 eV). UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-4100) and a spectrofluorometer (LS-55), respectively.

For determining Al3+, 1 mL of Al3+ aqueous solutions with different concentrations (0–90 μM) were mixed with 1 mL of RCD suspensions (0.06 mg/mL) and 1 mL of QCT solutions (240 μM) in ethanol under gentle shaking. After 5 min, the fluorescence spectra of the above mixtures were taken.

To evaluate the selectivity of the above probe, 1 mL of aqueous solutions containing 150 μM of different competing metal ions were mixed with 1 mL of RCD suspensions (0.06 mg/mL) and 1 mL of QCT solutions (240 μM) in ethanol under gentle shaking. After 5 min, the fluorescence spectra of the above mixtures were taken.

Fig. 1 a gives the TEM image of RCDs, reflecting the successful synthesis of RCDs with a narrow particle size distribution of 5.2 ± 2.5 nm (Fig. S1a) and nearly spherical morphology. The particle size distribution of RCD is calculated by counting more than 50 particles. The high-resolution TEM in Fig. 1b reveals that the RCDs possess an amorphous structure and smooth surface. Only one short and broad peak at 21.7° representing the (002) lattice plane of graphite can be observed in the XRD pattern (Fig. S1b), which further verifies the poor degree of crystallinity of RCDs.

To disclose the surface state of RCDs, the FTIR and XPS spectra were obtained and shown in Fig. S1c and Fig. S2, respectively. In the FTIR spectrum, the peaks at 3313 and 3198 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively. The peaks at 1615 and 1510 cm−1 are caused by the corporate stretching vibrations of CC, C=N and C = O. The peaks at 1386, 1115 and 833 cm−1 can be corresponding to the deformation vibrations of OH, stretching vibrations of CO and CN, and deformation vibrations of CH, respectively. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. S2a) shows that the RCDs are composed of C, N and O elements with atomic ratios of 80.8%, 14.8% and 4.4%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum shown in Fig. S2b indicates that the CC/C = C (284.6 eV), CO/CN (285.4 eV) and C = O (287.3 eV) species are existent on the surfaces of RCDs. The N1s XPS band (Fig. S2c) displays that the surface N elements of RCDs exist in the forms of pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV) and amino N (399.1 eV) species. The O1s XPS band (Fig. S2d) illustrates that the O elements on the RCD surfaces are composed of C = O (531.4 eV) and COC/COH (533.0 eV) species. The results of FTIR and XPS spectra corporately reveal that the surfaces of CDs are decorated by some amino groups and oxygen-containing groups. Meanwhile, lots of N-heterocycles such as the pyridinic and pyrrolic rings are also existent.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, there are two UV-visible absorption bands located at 260–350 and 400–600 nm. The former is corresponding to the π-π* electronic transitions of aromatic C = C bonds and n-π* transitions of C = N and C = O bonds [29]. The latter is attributed to the electronic transitions of surface N-heterocycles [46–48], which may also contribute to the fluorescence (FL) emission in red wavelength [33]. The FL excitation and emission spectra shown in Fig. 2a clearly reflect the obvious fluorescence (FL) phenomenon of RCDs in ethanol. The RCDs suspension shows brown color under room light but emits bright red fluorescence under UV light (inset of Fig. 2b), which further confirms the FL property of RCDs. Fig. 2b displays the excitation-independent FL behavior of RCDs, that is, the emission wavelength is invariable at 605 nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 320 to 540 nm. While the FL intensity is first enhanced and then reduced with increasing excitation wavelength, reaching a maximum upon 500 nm excitation. Using quinine sulfate as reference (Fig. S3), the fluorescence quantum yield of RCDs is still relatively high (about 9.6%) under 360 nm excitation. Fig. S4a and S4b displays that the FL intensity of RCDs is well related to their concentration (c RCDs) and the optimal c RCDs is 0.02 mg/mL. Moreover, the FL intensity of RCDs is high and stable in the wide pH range of 5–11 (Fig. S4c) and c NaCl range of 0–4 M, which is particular beneficial for their application in fluorescence determination.

The principle to construct the visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe for Al3+ is that the red fluorescence of RCDs (605 nm) can serve as the reference signal, and meanwhile the gradually strengthened green fluorescence owing to the continuous formation of Al3+-QCT complex (506 nm) with increasing c Al 3+ can be used as the reporter [18]. Fig. 3 reflects the particularly specific selectivity of RCD-QCT system to Al3+ based on the distinct features of big I506/I605 value and yellow green fluorescence color, which provides solid foundation for constructing the visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe of Al3+.

To determine the linear detection range and limit of detection (LOD) of the Al3+ probe, the FL spectra of RCDs-QCT system with different c Al 3+ under 360 nm excitation were obtained and shown in Fig. 4 a. Obviously, the red fluorescence at 605 nm is relatively stable while the green fluorescence at 506 nm gradually increases with increasing c Al 3+, accompanied by the fluorescence color change from red to yellow green (inset of Fig. 4a). Fig. 4b gives the curve of I506/I605 value varying with c Al 3+ and the fitted linear relationship between the I506/I605 value and c Al 3+. Based on the results in Fig. 4b, the detection range is confirmed as 0–35 μM with high degree of fitting (R2 = 0.996). According to the 3σ criteria, the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as 0.09 μM.

The anti-interference ability of Al3+ probe was also evaluated by using tap water to displace the ultrapure water in the determination system. As is well known, there are a lot of inorganic ions (such as K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, F−, Cl−, SO4 2−, etc.) in tap water. However, the results shown in Fig. 5
are similar to that in Fig. 4. The detection range for Al3+ in tap water system is 0–30 μM with the high degree of fitting (R2 = 0.993) and the LOD is 0.094 μM, reflecting the high anti-interference ability of the above Al3+ probe.

The performances of the CD-based Al3+ fluorescent probes are summarized in Table 1 . Compared with the single-signal probes for Al3+, the probe established here exhibits improved detection accuracy and stability. Compared with the previous ratiometric fluorescent probes, our probe using the red fluorescence of CDs as reference signal possess simple construction procedures, outstanding anti-interference ability, comparable detection range and relatively low LOD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery tests in ultrapure and tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The possibility of the above probe for practical application was evaluated by the recovery tests. The results shown in Table 2 demonstrate that the probe based on RCD-QCT system well realize quantitative determination of the spiked Al3+ in ultrapure and tap water samples with satisfactory recoveries and low RSD (SD/(the average result) × 100%), implying its great potential for practical fluorescent determination of Al3+ in various aqueous environments.

With increasing c Al 3+, the red fluorescence of RCD-QCT system under 360 nm excitation was almost stable, and meanwhile the green fluorescence was gradually enhanced due to the continuous formation of Al3+–QCT complex. Then using the red fluorescence as the reference signal and the green as the reporter, the RCD-QCT system was constructed as a visual and ratiometric fluorescent probe towards Al3+ based on the variation of I 506/I 605 value and the change of fluorescence color from red to yellow green. The detection range and LOD of the probe were determined as 0–35 μM and 0.09 μM, respectively. The probe established here not only showed the high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability but also realized the quantitative determination of Al3+ in ultrapure and tap water samples with satisfactory results, suggesting its great application potential for the practical fluorescent determination of Al3+ in various aqueous environments.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493) and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project of China(2015M582567).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104807.
